title: State-building: technology and cultural transfers during the Crusades
description: Read passages and answer questions about technology and cultural transfers during the Crusades.
topic_path: State-building: technology and cultural transfers during the Crusades

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, read the following passage:

> "And, perhaps most of all, the crusades brought many more Europeans than before into close contact with Muslims and Byzantines. It is no mere coincidence that Arabic was first studied in Europe in the twelfth century or, at the same time, European libraries began to fill up with books that had been long lost to Western scholars, but were now “found” in Muslim and Byzantine libraries and translated into Latin."

From Judith M. Bennett and C. Warren Holister. _Medieval Europe: A Short History_ (New York: McGraw Hill, 2006), 234-235. 

What is Bennett and Hollister’s argument about the impact of the crusades on Europe? 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Many classical Greek and Roman texts had been translated and expanded on by Muslim scholars in the centuries prior to the crusades.

choices.content: The increased contact between Europeans, Byzantines, and Muslims led to knowledge being (re)introduced into Europe
Closer contacts with Byzantines and Muslims made Europeans suspicious of them and less open to new knowledge from these societies
Many Europeans adopted Arabic as an official language
The crusaders stole many books from the Byzantines and Muslims, leading to improved knowledge

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, read the following passage:

> "Culturally Frankish society in the East remained basically provincial and thoroughly dependent on Europe. The Islamic influence which was so fruitful in Spain and Sicily was rigidly excluded in Outremer [Crusader states in the Holy Land]. The Franks coexisted with the Muslims but there was no symbiosis [cultural blending]. The number of those from the upper ranks of society who bothered to learn Arabic was tiny. Their everyday language (...) was French. (...) Only in superficial matters, dress, medicine, and domestic comforts was there any willingness to learn from Islam. "

From Hans Eberhard Mayer, _The Crusades_ (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1988), 189.

What is Mayer’s argument about cross-cultural influences during the crusades? 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Cross-cultural influences tend to vary by specific location.

choices.content: Europeans everywhere were always resistant to cross-cultural influences
Europeans in Outremer were resistant to cross-cultural influences
Europeans in Outremer adopted many Islamic customs and abandoned much of European culture
Many Muslims in Outremer adopted European customs

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, read the following passages:

> "And, perhaps most of all, the crusades brought many more Europeans than before into close contact with Muslims and Byzantines. It is no mere coincidence that Arabic was first studied in Europe in the twelfth century or, at the same time, European libraries began to fill up with books that had been long lost to Western scholars, but were now “found” in Muslim and Byzantine libraries and translated into Latin."

From Judith M. Bennett and C. Warren Holister. _Medieval Europe: A Short History_ (New York: McGraw Hill, 2006), 234-235. 

> "Culturally Frankish society in the East remained basically provincial and thoroughly dependent on Europe. The Islamic influence which was so fruitful in Spain and Sicily was rigidly excluded in Outremer [Crusader states in the Holy Land]. The Franks coexisted with the Muslims but there was no symbiosis [cultural blending]. The number of those from the upper ranks of society who bothered to learn Arabic was tiny. Their everyday language (...) was French. (...) Only in superficial matters, dress, medicine, and domestic comforts was there any willingness to learn from Islam. "

From Hans Eberhard Mayer, _The Crusades_ (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1988), 189.

Which of the following is a historical claim that could be supported by using both Bennett and Hollister’s argument and Mayer’s argument? 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Cross-cultural influences tend to vary by specific location.

choices.content: Europeans everywhere readily embraced customs they encountered among Muslims and Byzantines during the crusades
Cross-cultural exchange during the crusades was more impactful in Europe itself than in Outremer
European culture and attitudes were largely unaffected by cross-cultural exchanges during the crusades
Many crusaders converted to Islam after living in the Holy Land

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question below, read the following passage:

> "The intellectual and physical, geographic aspects of the crusade’s influence on European expansion cannot neatly be separated. Neither should it be exaggerated. The creation of Asiatic empires and the altering of trade routes; the development of the European economy, technology and commerce; or the transmission of classical and Arabic texts via Spain, Sicily, southern Italy and Byzantium ran distinct and parallel to the effects of the wars of the cross. However, crusading idealism led to significant political settlements of Latin Christians in the Near East and, in places, an obsessive European concern for western Asia and the Eastern Mediterranean that would not have developed in the way it did with the distinctive dynamism of the crusade mentality and tradition. Politically, the nature of the Muslim powers of the Near East mattered to Frankish rulers and warriors." 

From Christopher Tyerman, _God’s War: A New History of the Crusades_ (Cambridge: Belknap Press, 2006), 912-913.

What is Tyerman’s argument about the impacts of the crusades?  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Especially in the Near East, the crusades were largely a religious and political movement.

choices.content: Many influences on Europe during and after the crusades were direct results of the crusades themselves
The most important impacts of the crusades on Europe were economic
The crusades made Europeans less interested in affairs outside of Europe
The biggest direct impact of the crusades was increased European interest in Near Eastern politics
